text: The surgical team sets up the operating table with four ports, each measuring 8.5 millimeters, and upsizes one port for the stapled radial arm. The robotic arms are being docked to the ports.
The surgeon exposes the right upper quadrant and identifies the ileocecal area, revealing the distended cecum that appears twisted.

The surgeon navigates to the right upper quadrant, identifying the hepatic flexure. Suction and irrigation are used to remove fluid from the pelvis, while a 4x4 gauze is employed for gentle retraction.
The surgeon uses a purse-string suture to decompress the distended cecum, employing a 3-0 vicryl suture and suction to relieve tension in the colon, facilitating bowel manipulation.

The surgeon begins the division and lateral mobilization of the ascending colon and cecum, using the robotic fourth arm for retraction and exposure. The mesentery appears redundant, aiding in the controlled manipulation of the bowel.

The surgeon carefully retracts and exposes the surgical field to avoid major spillage, using a vessel sealer to divide the mesentery. The area for proximal division of the distal ileum is marked, and a window is created.
The surgeon approaches the base of the mesentery near the colon, preparing to divide it close to the hepatic flexure.

The surgeon performs medial to lateral dissection for the division of the mesentery, with proper retraction revealing the duodenum in the right lower corner of the view. A window is created to prepare the distal margin for transection.
The surgeon uses the robotic system's fourth arm to achieve optimal exposure and retraction of the surgical field, eliminating the need for laparoscopic trocars or additional assistance.
The surgeon completes the mobilization of the transverse colon in the right upper quadrant, preparing for the transection of the colon.

The surgeon uses a 45 blue load stapler followed by another 45 blue stapler to divide the colon. Indocyanine green (ICG) is administered intravenously to visually confirm perfusion and ensure the integrity of the divided section.

The surgeon places a suture for retraction and makes a colotomy with scissors, visibly decompressing the colon. Another enterotomy is made with scissors, with controlled manipulation using robotic arms and instruments to prevent spillage into the peritoneal cavity.
The surgeon sutures the common channel using a 3-0 barbed suture in a running fashion, employing an outside-in, inside-out technique. The suture is visibly nine inches in length.

The robotic fourth arm provides optimal exposure for the anastomosis site. Initially, the sutures are tightened, but as the procedure progresses, the opening is left visible to ensure proper closure. Indocyanine green (ICG) or Motan may be used towards the end to confirm adequate blood supply to the anastomosis.
The surgeon completes the closure of the anastomosis, locking the sutures in place to secure the connection.
The surgeon removes the resected colon through an Alexis wound protector at the stapler port. A drain is placed next to the anastomosis site.
